title: Applying PVA primer to bare plaster before tiling
subtitle: Seal new or patched plaster so tile adhesive bonds reliably
excerpt: How to apply a diluted PVA primer to bare plaster before tiling, reducing suction and giving tile adhesive a stable surface to key onto. Covers mixing ratios, application, drying time, and how to identify under-primed patches before laying begins.
sourceNotes: BS 5385-1: Wall and floor tiling (ceramic tiles, Part 1). British Gypsum White Book (plastering and substrate preparation). City and Guilds Level 2 Tiling. Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics.

glossaryTerms.definition: The rate at which a porous substrate absorbs moisture from adhesive or mortar. High-suction substrates (bare sand-and-cement render, new plasterboard, fresh plaster) draw water out of adhesive before it has time to bond, causing early dry-out and weak adhesion. Reducing suction with a primer gives adhesive time to cure at full strength.

A water-based adhesive and primer used in construction to reduce substrate suction and improve bonding. Diluted with water for priming (approximately 1 part PVA to 4 parts water for plaster); used neat as a bonding additive in plaster mixes. Turns from milky to clear as it dries.

content.text: substrate: it will pull the water out of tile adhesive faster than the adhesive can cure, leading to weak bonds and early tile failure. A diluted

coat seals the surface pores enough for the adhesive to set properly. New skim-coat plaster, bonding coat, and any patched areas all need this treatment before tiling begins. Sand-and-cement render is lower-suction but still benefits from priming; old painted surfaces are the exception and need a different preparation entirely.

PVA adhesive (standard construction grade, sold in 1 litre and 5 litre bottles). Clean water. A bucket or container for mixing. A 50 mm paintbrush or a 9-inch roller for large areas. Sandpaper (120 grit) for knocking back any high spots or ridges on the plaster surface.
Step 1, check and prepare the plaster surface

The plaster must be fully cured before priming. New skim-coat plaster is ready when it has turned from grey to a uniform pale cream; this takes at least four to six weeks in a warm, ventilated room. Priming too early traps moisture and leads to hollow-sounding tiles within months. Run the sanding block across the surface to knock back any ridges, trowel lines, or proud patches; the face must be flat enough to receive the adhesive layer uniformly.

Dilute the PVA at 1 part PVA to 4 parts clean water for skim-coat plaster and 1 part PVA to 3 parts water for bonding coat or sand-and-cement render. Stir the solution well; PVA separates when left to stand. Pour only as much as you will use in one pass, as the diluted mix starts to skin over within a couple of hours in a warm room.

Brush or roll the PVA solution evenly across the entire tiling area, working from top to bottom. Do not leave puddles or dry patches; a thin, even coat is the target. The solution will look milky white as it goes on. Where the PVA is absorbed very quickly and the surface looks dry within seconds of application, allow the first coat to cure, then apply a second coat to the same area before tiling. These are the highest-suction spots and need more sealing.
Step 4, allow to cure and check before tiling

The PVA coat must be fully cured before any adhesive goes on. Full cure is 2 to 4 hours in a warm room; the surface should be clear, lightly tacky but no longer wet. Do not tile onto a wet or still-milky PVA surface: it forms a slippery parting layer and the tiles will slide or fail to bond. Check by pressing a tile gently against the surface: it should grip slightly without sliding. If the tile slides freely, the coat is still wet; leave it longer.

On plasterboard (rather than plaster), dilute PVA at 1:3 to wet down the paper face. Let it fully cure before applying adhesive; plasterboard face paper is also a high-suction surface. Some adhesive manufacturers specify their own branded primer instead of PVA. If the adhesive data sheet recommends this, use it; many adhesive warranties depend on the correct primer being used.

Old paint is not treated with PVA. Painted walls need the paint tested for adhesion first: score a cross-hatch with a utility knife and press masking tape over it firmly, then pull the tape sharply. If paint lifts with the tape, the paint layer must be removed before tiling. If it holds, rub the surface with 80-grit paper to key it and use a specialist tile primer rather than diluted PVA.

attrs.symptom: Tiles slide down the wall when the adhesive is still wet
attrs.cause: PVA coat not fully cured; adhesive is not gripping the surface.
attrs.fix: Remove the tiles. Allow the PVA to cure fully until clear and lightly tacky. Apply a thin layer of adhesive with the flat face of the trowel before the notched-trowel layer; this double-coat method increases initial grip while the adhesive sets.

attrs.symptom: Adhesive skins over rapidly and the back of tiles shows poor coverage
attrs.cause: Suction still too high; PVA coat too dilute or one coat insufficient.
attrs.fix: Apply a second, slightly less dilute coat (1:3 ratio) and allow to cure. Work in smaller areas so the adhesive does not skin before the tile is pressed home.

attrs.symptom: Hollow-sounding tiles within weeks of laying
attrs.cause: Plaster was not fully cured when PVA was applied; moisture trapped behind tiles has caused debonding.
attrs.fix: Tile and adhesive will need to be removed. Allow the plaster to cure completely (4 to 6 weeks minimum for skim), re-prime, and re-tile.
